body_txt: "Cover version" is music industry lingo for the common practice of an artist creating their own version of a song that was first produced and released by someone else. They're also sometimes called a "remake", but since that can also refer to an artist re-recording their own song, "cover version" is the more unambiguous, standardized term. It's often shortened to "cover" and "cover" is also used as a verb meaning to perform a cover version. Up until The '50s , popular music was based more around songs than artists. Many different artists would record and release competing versions of a new song around the same time. After the end of World War II , Rhythm and Blues and Country Music started to gain crossover interest outside of their niche audiences, and as a result, competing record labels often recorded and released their own versions of these songs, usually done in a more homogenized mainstream pop style. The term "cover version" originated to describe this practice, though there are some competing explanations as to why the word "cover" was used. One is that the label who released the competing version was trying to "cover up" the existence of the original so they could get more sales of the song. The more likely origin is that these labels were seeking to "cover the market" (or "cover the bases") by releasing versions of songs outside of their usual genres. Black artists often found themselves having to compete with "white" versions of their songs, with the unspoken subtext that many white Americans would refuse to listen to a song by a black artist. Most famously, Pat Boone made a career out of recording versions of songs by the likes of Little Richard and Fats Domino . But with the rise of Rock & Roll (and desegregation) audiences vastly preferred the original versions to the covers, so the practice largely died out, except for situations like versions of songs from one genre aimed at audiences of another genre (up until The '80s it was common to hear country renditions of pop hits, or R&B takes on country songs), or performers in places like Australia and New Zealand doing local versions of British and American hits (so they could hit the market before the originals could be imported to their countries). Instead, the cover version became an artistic statement, with an artist doing a their own take of a song as a sign of their admiration for it, out of a sense that it fit their own style well, or with a feeling that they could add something unique to a pre-existing song in their performance of it. Usually it's easy to define a cover: if you hear a singer or band doing a new version of a song made famous by another singer or band, it's a cover. Some instances are murkier. Sometimes a Singer-Songwriter will write a song, but let another artist record and release it first, like when The Beatles gave "I Wanna Be Your Man" to The Rolling Stones before they finished their own version. Songs written by non-performing songwriters (like the classic Tin Pan Alley and Brill Building composers) are also kind of tricky as to pinning down what could be considered the "original" and what could be a cover. With "White Christmas", Irving Berlin wrote it specifically for Bing Crosby to sing in Holiday Inn , so it's easy to call it a Bing Crosby song. "Twist and Shout" was first recorded by an obscure group called The Top Notes, but it was The Isley Brothers who first had a hit with it, so it's fair to call any subsequent version a cover of the Isleys. Hit songs tend to attract cover versions, on the basic logic that if the public liked the original version, odds are they'll like another version of the same song. If the cover version becomes more successful, it has Covered Up the original. If a cover is done in another language then it's a Translated Cover Version . For specific types of cover see also The Cover Changes the Gender , The Cover Changes the Meaning , and Softer and Slower Cover . If you're curious, the website WhoSampled has a quite thorough list of the most popular songs to cover. An entire album being composed of cover songs is a Cover Album . There's also the ubiquitous "cover band", a group that exclusively performs cover versions (often heard at amusement parks, weddings, birthday parties and bar mitzvahs ), with a "tribute band" being a group that exclusively covers songs by a particular band, which in some cases also mixes with Celebrity Impersonator . Compare and contrast with remix , a related concept that is more common in electronic music. Differences in the production process:    Differences in ownership:    It's also worth nothing that some versions that are officially released as remixes might technically be covers, when the remixing artist chooses not to re-use any of the provided parts of the original work and only uses the melody. Likewise, some versions that are officially released as covers can actually sample some parts of the original. This can happen when the owner of the original enjoys the remix and has no problem with letting it see the light of day, but does not want to release it as a remix. A common reason for this is when the remix is in a wildly different style than the original artist is known for, and their label doesn't want to feature this work in the artist's official discography. In that case, they can allow the remixing artist to release the remix as a cover, and additionally allow the artist to sample the original work, as a separate legal technicality. See Above & Beyond - Blue Monday for a prominent example.
